title: Fan into Flame the Gift of God

sections.header: Verses 1–7 — "I Remind You to Fan into Flame the Gift of God"

heb.gloss: to fan into flame, to rekindle
heb.paragraph: A vivid compound verb found only here in the NT. The metaphor pictures a fire that has died down to embers and needs to be stirred back to full blaze. BDAG gives 'to cause to be more alive or fervent.' The implication is not that Timothy has lost his gift but that the pressures of the Ephesian situation have dampened his zeal. The gift was conferred through the laying on of Paul's hands (cf. 1 Tim 4:14).

The noun denotes craven fear or cowardice. Paul's point is that the Holy Spirit's indwelling produces not timidity but power, love, and self-discipline—three qualities Timothy will need as opposition intensifies.

panels.ctx: 2 Timothy is widely regarded as the most personal of Paul's letters, written from a Roman prison with the expectation of death (4:6–8). Paul remembers Timothy's tears (v. 4), recalls his grandmother Lois and mother Eunice (v. 5), and tenderly exhorts him to courage. The emphasis on power vs. timidity suggests Timothy was under significant pressure in Ephesus.

cross.note: The parallel ordination reference describes the same event but emphasizes the presbytery's collective participation.
Paul's initial recruitment of Timothy in Lystra provides the biographical backdrop for the familial language here.
The contrast between the spirit of slavery/fear and the Spirit of adoption parallels the contrast here between timidity and power.

notes.note: Paul writes as an apostle by the will of God. Timothy is his dear son. Every word breathes paternal affection and apostolic authority.
Paul thanks God for Timothy and remembers him constantly in prayers. The sincere faith that lives in Timothy first lived in his grandmother Lois and mother Eunice—multigenerational faithfulness.
Fan into flame the gift of God! God has not given us a spirit of timidity but of power, love, and self-discipline. These three qualities are the antidote to every pastoral failure.
Paul's thanksgiving overflows with personal affection. The mention of Timothy's grandmother and mother demonstrates that faith is best transmitted through the household.

The fire metaphor warns that spiritual gifts can be banked down by fear. The spirit God gives is not one of cowardice but of power for service, love for people, and self-discipline for personal holiness.
The reference to Lois and Eunice provides the only NT evidence for multigenerational faith as a background for ministry.
The triad of power, love, and self-discipline replaces the feared timidity and comprehensively equips Timothy for courageous ministry.
The emotional warmth exceeds any other Pauline letter. The genealogy of faith through Lois and Eunice demonstrates the sincere, intergenerational, tested faith Paul values.
The rekindle imperative is present tense, implying ongoing effort. The gift received at ordination requires human cooperation—a Pauline tension between grace and responsibility.

The multigenerational faith chain validates Timothy's credentials by tracing his spiritual formation to domestic origins, consistent with the Pastorals' emphasis on the household as the locus of authentic faith.

The fire metaphor addresses the real challenge of sustaining ministerial courage under pressure. The three Spirit-given qualities correspond to Timothy's three spheres of challenge: hostile opposition, fractured community, and personal stability.

sections.header: Verses 8–14 — "Do Not Be Ashamed of the Testimony"

heb.paragraph: The deposit metaphor appears three times across the Pastorals (1 Tim 6:20; 2 Tim 1:12, 14). In v. 12 Paul expresses confidence that God will guard 'my deposit.' In v. 14, Timothy is charged to guard the 'good deposit' through the Holy Spirit. The gospel is received, guarded, and transmitted—not invented or adapted.
The noun denotes a model or pattern to be imitated. Paul's 'sound words' are the pattern of healthy teaching—conveying both the content and the form of apostolic instruction.

panels.ctx: Paul's exhortation not to be ashamed takes on sharp urgency given his imprisonment. The creedal fragment in vv. 9–10 is widely recognized as pre-Pauline—a salvation summary moving from eternal purpose through historical manifestation to eschatological hope. Paul's claim to be appointed herald, apostle, and teacher grounds his authority in divine commission.

cross.note: Paul's declaration that he is not ashamed of the gospel provides the theological foundation for the same exhortation to Timothy.
Jesus' warning that whoever is ashamed of him will face eschatological consequences adds christological weight.
The pre-temporal election language parallels the creedal fragment's 'before the beginning of time.'

notes.note: Do not be ashamed of the testimony about our Lord, or of Paul his prisoner. God saved us and called us with a holy calling—not because of works but because of his own purpose and grace.
This grace was given before the beginning of time but has now been revealed through Christ's appearing, who destroyed death and brought life and immortality to light.
Paul was appointed herald, apostle, and teacher. He is not ashamed because he knows whom he has believed and is convinced God is able to guard what has been entrusted to him.
Hold fast the pattern of sound teaching. Guard the good deposit entrusted to you through the Holy Spirit who lives in us.
Paul calls Timothy to share in suffering, grounding the appeal in God's eternal purpose. Suffering for the gospel is participation in God's saving work.
Paul's confidence rests not on his own strength but on God's faithfulness. Timothy must hold fast to the pattern and guard the deposit through the Spirit.
The creedal fragment has a pre-Pauline character, using vocabulary uncommon in the undisputed letters.
The phrase 'what I have entrusted to him' is ambiguous: it may mean Paul's life/ministry entrusted to God, or the gospel entrusted to Paul.
The shame language addresses the social cost of identifying with an imprisoned apostle. The creedal fragment moves from pre-temporal purpose through historical appearing to triumph over death.

The herald/apostle/teacher triad defines Paul's commission in three dimensions. The deposit in v. 12 is matched by the 'good deposit' of v. 14, creating a reciprocal structure: God guards Paul's deposit, Timothy guards God's.
The shame/suffering exhortation situates Timothy's challenge within the Roman social context. The creedal fragment reframes suffering as participation in a divine drama that precedes time.
The deposit language creates a chain of custody: from God through Paul to Timothy through the Holy Spirit. This is pneumatological transmission—the Spirit actively enables faithful preservation.

sections.header: Verses 15–18 — "Onesiphorus Was Not Ashamed of My Chains"

heb.paragraph: A concrete marker of Paul's imprisonment. Onesiphorus's willingness to seek out Paul in Rome and identify with a chained prisoner exemplifies the courage Timothy is being exhorted to show. The shame of chains in the Roman world was profound: imprisonment marked one as a criminal and social outcast.

panels.ctx: The contrast between Phygelus and Hermogenes (who deserted Paul) and Onesiphorus (who actively sought him out in Rome) provides concrete examples of the shame/courage dynamic. Paul's second imprisonment was apparently more severe than his first, and many associates distanced themselves.

cross.note: Paul's self-designation as 'a prisoner of Christ Jesus' demonstrates the theological reframing of imprisonment as a badge of apostolic service.
The exhortation to remember prisoners 'as if chained with them' reflects the same ethic of solidarity that Onesiphorus exemplified.

notes.note: Everyone in the province of Asia has deserted Paul. When pressure mounts, fair-weather friends disappear.
But Onesiphorus was not ashamed of Paul's chains. He searched hard for Paul in Rome and found him. Paul prays the Lord will show mercy to him on that final day.

The desertion by Asian Christians reveals the cost of faithful witness. Onesiphorus stands as a shining contrast—he was not ashamed of the chains and sought Paul out diligently. True fellowship extends to the suffering, not only to the prosperous.
The blanket statement that 'everyone in Asia' deserted Paul is hyperbolic, highlighting the severity of Paul's isolation.
The prayer for mercy 'on that day' has been debated: some take it as evidence Onesiphorus had died; others argue he was still alive.

The Phygelus/Hermogenes vs. Onesiphorus contrast exemplifies the letter's central theme: faithfulness vs. defection under pressure. The eschatological prayer ('on that day') is a standard Pauline reference to the Day of the Lord.

The contrast section functions as social exemplification: two named deserters against one named loyalist. Onesiphorus's behavior concretizes the abstract exhortation of v. 8: this is what 'not being ashamed' looks like in practice.

themes.note: Faith dominates as the chapter centers on fidelity to the gospel deposit under pressure. Sovereignty and Mercy emerge through the pre-temporal salvation plan.

rows.label: vv.3–7
rows.text: Thanksgiving, family of faith, rekindle the gift

rows.label: vv.8–14
rows.text: Suffering, creedal confession, guard the deposit

lit.note: Chapter 1 establishes the dominant themes: shame vs. courage, the deposit of teaching, and endurance for the gospel. Personal affection (vv. 1–7), theological grounding (vv. 8–14), and concrete examples (vv. 15–18).

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Timothy
ppl.role: Paul’s young protégé; co-author of six NT letters
ppl.text: Timothy is recruited at Lystra on the second journey (Acts 16:1–3) — the son of a Jewish mother and Greek father, circumcised by Paul to aid Jewish outreach. He becomes Paul’s most trusted emissary, sent to troubled churches when Paul cannot go. Paul twice calls him "my true son in the faith" and addresses two personal letters to him. He is co-author of six Pauline letters and appears at the end of Hebrews.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Fan into Flame the Gift of God

translations.text: He has saved us and called us to a holy life—not because of anything we have done but because of his own purpose and grace. This grace was given us in Christ Jesus before the beginning of time,
who saved us and called us to a holy calling, not because of our works but because of his own purpose and grace, which he gave us in Christ Jesus before the ages began,
For the Spirit God gave us does not make us timid, but gives us power, love and self-discipline.
for God gave us a spirit not of fear but of power and love and self-control.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Paul writes as an apostle by the will of God. Timothy is his dear son. Every word breathes paternal affection and apostolic authority."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Paul's thanksgiving overflows with personal affection. The mention of Timothy's grandmother and mother demonstrates that faith is best transmitted through the household."

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The reference to Lois and Eunice provides the only NT evidence for multigenerational faith as a background for ministry."

debate: The creedal fragment has a pre-Pauline character, using vocabulary uncommon in the undisputed letters.…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Fan into Flame the Gift of God within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
The prayer for mercy 'on that day' has been debated: some take it as evidence Onesiphorus had died; others argue he was still alive.…
